2603.10541.title: Prompting with the human-touch: evaluating model-sensitivity of foundation models for musculoskeletal CT segmentation
2603.10541.paper_summary: Promptable Foundation Models (FMs), initially introduced for natural image segmentation, have also revolutionized medical image segmentation. The increasing number of models, along with evaluations varying in datasets, metrics, and compared models, makes direct performance comparison between models difficult and complicates the selection of the most suitable model for specific clinical tasks. In our study, 11 promptable FMs are tested using non-iterative 2D and 3D prompting strategies on a private and public dataset focusing on bone and implant segmentation in four anatomical regions (wrist, shoulder, hip and lower leg). The Pareto-optimal models are identified and further analyzed using human prompts collected through a dedicated observer study. Our findings are: 1) The segmentation performance varies a lot between FMs and prompting strategies; 2) The Pareto-optimal models in 2D are SAM and SAM2.1, in 3D nnInteractive and Med-SAM2; 3) Localization accuracy and rater consistency vary with anatomical structures, with higher consistency for simple structures (wrist bones) and lower consistency for complex structures (pelvis, tibia, implants); 4) The segmentation performance drops using human prompts, suggesting that performance reported on "ideal" prompts extracted from reference labels might overestimate the performance in a human-driven setting; 5) All models were sensitive to prompt variations. While two models demonstrated intra-rater robustness, it did not scale to inter-rater settings. We conclude that the selection of the most optimal FM for a human-driven setting remains challenging, with even high-performing FMs being sensitive to variations in human input prompts. Our code base for prompt extraction and model inference is available: https://github.com/CarolineMagg/segmentation-FM-benchmark/
2603.10541.paper_summary_zh: Promptable Foundation Models (FMs) 最初是為了自然圖像分割而引入的，但也徹底改變了醫學圖像分割。模型數量的增加，加上在數據集、指標和比較模型方面的評估變化，使得模型之間的直接性能比較變得困難，並使得選擇最適合特定臨床任務的模型變得複雜。在我們的研究中，使用非迭代的 2D 和 3D 提示策略對 11 個可提示的 FMs 進行測試，數據集包括專有和公共數據集，重點是四個解剖區域（手腕、肩膀、髖部和小腿）的骨骼和植入物分割。確定了 Pareto 最優模型，並進一步使用通過專門觀察者研究收集的人類提示進行分析。我們的發現是：1) FMs 和提示策略之間的分割性能差異很大；2) 2D 中的 Pareto 最優模型是 SAM 和 SAM2.1，3D 中是 nnInteractive 和 Med-SAM2；3) 定位準確性和評估者一致性隨著解剖結構的不同而變化，對於簡單結構（手腕骨）的一致性較高，而對於複雜結構（骨盆、脛骨、植入物）的一致性較低；4) 使用人類提示時，分割性能下降，這表明在從參考標籤提取的“理想”提示上報告的性能可能會高估在以人為驅動的環境中的性能；5) 所有模型對提示變化都很敏感。雖然兩個模型顯示出評估者內部的穩健性，但這並未擴展到評估者之間的設置。我們得出結論，為以人為驅動的環境選擇最優的 FM 仍然具有挑戰性，即使是高性能的 FMs 也對人類輸入提示的變化敏感。我們的提示提取和模型推斷的代碼庫可用於：https://github.com/CarolineMagg/segmentation-FM-benchmark/
2603.10541.author: Caroline Magg et.al.
2603.10541.authors: Caroline Magg, Maaike A. ter Wee, Johannes G. G. Dobbe, Geert J. Streekstra, Leendert Blankevoort, Clara I. Sánchez, Hoel Kervadec
